serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of ginger sauce chicken with rice is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice topped with braised ginger chicken pieces and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken (braised) and cooked white rice
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete dish 'Cơm gà kho gừng'. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of: (1) FAO/INFOODS SEA table entries for cooked white rice (~60% by weight) and braised chicken (~35% by weight), and (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised chicken thigh (NDB 05165) and cooked long-grain white rice (NDB 20045), with minor contributions from ginger, soy sauce, and fish sauce (~5% by weight). Final per-100g values reflect the composite dish at approximately 60% rice and 35% chicken by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish Cơm gà kho gừng. GI estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: boiled white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database; mean 64 used for jasmine-type rice). The chicken and ginger braising sauce contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 59.85) / 100 ≈ 38.3, rounded to 38.4. Carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 13.3 × 4.5 = 59.85 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g (1800 mg per standard 450 g serving), primarily from fish sauce and soy sauce in the braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce in the braising liquid, opt for low-sodium soy sauce, and avoid adding extra condiments at the table, as a single 450 g serving can contribute approximately 78% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~38 per serving) driven by white rice (GI ~64) with low dietary fibre (0.7 g per 100 g), promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consider substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycemic load, limit the portion to half a standard plate, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (400 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) from chicken skin and cooking oil, with a favourable lean-protein contribution from chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose skinless chicken breast to reduce saturated fat, request reduced-sodium condiments, and ensure the overall meal is balanced with vegetables to support a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger (gingerols) provides evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit, but high sodium from fish sauce and soy sauce may worsen RA-related tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The ginger content is beneficial for joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; however, patients should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and moderate white rice portions to avoid glycaemic-driven systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm gà kho gừng (Ginger Sauce Chicken with Rice) is a classic Vietnamese home-style dish consisting of tender chicken pieces braised in a fragrant, savoury ginger sauce, served over steamed white rice. The braising liquid is built from fresh ginger, fish sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and black pepper, producing a deeply aromatic, slightly caramelised sauce that coats the chicken. The chicken — typically thigh or drumstick pieces — is first seared then slow-braised until the meat is fall-off-the-bone tender and the sauce reduces to a glossy, concentrated glaze. The dish is served as a complete one-plate meal with a generous mound of steamed jasmine or long-grain white rice that absorbs the braising sauce. Nutritionally, a 450 g serving provides approximately 648 kcal, 45 g protein, 25 g fat, and 60 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 400 mg per 100 g (approximately 1800 mg per serving) due to fish sauce and soy sauce. The glycemic load per serving is approximately 38, driven by the white rice component (GI ~64). Clinically, this dish is of concern for hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycemic load, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (sodium and saturated fat). The ginger content provides anti-inflammatory gingerols that are beneficial for conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free. It is commonly found in Vietnamese home cooking, Vietnamese restaurants, and food delivery platforms across Southeast Asia and Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete dish Cơm gà kho gừng. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the component foods (cooked white rice and braised chicken). The composite estimate is internally consistent and plausible, but cannot be rated 'high' confidence without a direct approved-source measurement of the complete dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice and braised chicken; no single approved-source entry exists for the complete dish. 2. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available from approved sources for this composite dish): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this specific Vietnamese braised dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for braised chicken thigh and are approximate. 4. potassium_mg (180.0) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for braised chicken and cooked white rice; this is an approximation. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (boiled white rice, University of Sydney GI Database). 6. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 5.6, carbohydrate_g 13.3) are non-zero and supported by the composite source estimate; no zero-macro justification is required.
